prevalence.source: Orphanet; European amyloidosis registries and cohort studies
prevalence.trend: increasing diagnosed prevalence due to better awareness and access to genetics and imaging

incidence.value: Rare; family-based detection and referral dominate

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare) but high per-patient morbidity and mortality without disease-modifying therapy
burden.mortalityRate: Meaningful without therapy; reduced with earlier diagnosis and treatment
burden.source: Registry outcomes and WHO Europe chronic disease context

riskFactors: delayed diagnosis due to multisystem presentation
vulnerablePopulations: patients with cardiomyopathy plus red flags
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Genetic confirmation (TTR), cardiac imaging and scintigraphy protocols, biopsy with amyloid typing when needed; multidisciplinary neurology/cardiology assessment

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no population screening; cascade testing in families and targeted evaluation of suspected cases
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Tens of thousands across EU (rare; underdiagnosed historically)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate to high where therapies and specialist pathways are accessible

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Specialist amyloidosis centers and referral networks exist in many countries
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Disease-modifying therapy (TTR stabilizers/silencers) per national reimbursement, heart failure and neuropathy management, genetic counseling/cascade testing
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Cross-country differences in reimbursement and access to high-cost therapies
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Strong specialist networks, genetics and imaging access, and increasing availability of disease-modifying therapies
